question: At the Last Supper, why do the covenant words (‘blood of the covenant’) matter for defending sacrificial atonement against ‘God can just forgive without anything’ objections?

choices.A: They invoke covenant-making blood language from Exodus and the promised new covenant, framing forgiveness as covenantal sacrifice rather than arbitrary dismissal.
choices.B: They mean the meal is only symbolic and unrelated to forgiveness.
choices.C: They deny that sin has consequences.
choices.D: They teach that God needs human blood to forgive.

teaching.title: Covenant-blood logic
teaching.body: ‘Blood of the covenant’ echoes Exodus’ covenant ratification and aligns with new covenant promises. In that biblical logic, forgiveness is not God shrugging at evil; it’s covenant repair through sacrificial self-giving. That’s a strong reply to the claim that atonement is unnecessary or incoherent.
